metadata.source_note: LODA publications library print of the VUP Operational Manual - Safety Net Components for FY2024/25 (56-page scanned image PDF with no embedded text layer, so extraction runs a fresh OCR at 350 dpi, eng, psm 3, over the whole file as a single block; the byte-identical file is also published in the FY2023/24 folder). Grounds the Direct Support household entitlement table: number of household members 1 pays FRW 7,500; 2 pays 12,000; 3 pays 15,000; 4 pays 18,000; 5 or more pays 21,000 - monthly transfers, framed by the manual's own text on monthly payment access. The RWAMOD v2.0 country report (May 2026) states this schedule is unchanged as of July 2025, so it stands for the CY2025 validation year. VUP Direct Support is administrative (LODA/MINALOC); no gazetted instrument fixes the schedule. Flag: scanned print, fresh 350 dpi OCR.

0. Introduction

The Vision Umurenge Programme was established in 2008 under the Ministry of Local Government (MINALOC). Originally established as a flagship programme within the first and second Economic Development and Poverty Reduction Strategy (EDPRS), the VUP remains a key mechanism for the delivery of a range of national targets under National Strategy for Transformation (NST !-2018-2024). VUP program has 4 components namely:

= Safety Nets,

=» Graduation component

= Shock responsive component

# Sensitisation and Public Communications. This operational manual covers the Safety Net component’s schemes including classic Public Works (cPW), expanded Public Works Labor based (ePVV) expanded Public Works service based: Community/ Home Based Early Child Development (C/HBECD), Nutrition Sensitive Direct Support (NSDS) Old Age Grant (OAG) and Direct Support (DS).

0.1. Establish Annual Targets Annual targets for all safety nets will be established from the Sector Strategic Plan and the guidance in the Planning and Budgeting Call Circular. The targets will include:

= Geographic scale up/coverage (the sectors where the safety nets will be implemented)

= The number of recipients to be covered and the % of eligible recipients to be covered in

each sector; = The transfer value and other costs # For cPW the number of working days for each recipient

0.2. Establish Geographic Scale Up Plans

DS and OAG are at national coverage and these will remain. cPW, ePW-LB and ePW- H/CBECD are not in all sectors but will be scaled up as resource allows.

The number of sectors covered within a district depends on proportion of national caseload of extremely poor households residing in a district according to recent EICV and regular data updates from the Social Protection Systems, as shown in Box I.

Box |: Summary of methodology for identifying VUP public works sectors The number of sectors targeted for VUP in a district shall be determined with reference to the

proportion of extremely poor households living in that district as follows:

Number of people living in extreme poverty in district (latest EICV data/SR) X Total nbr of sectors targeted for VUP PW Total number of people living in extreme poverty in Rwanda (EICV/SR)

« During scale up of ePW-H/CBECD, sectors with highest number of households in extreme poverty with children aged 0-5 will be prioritised in each district.

= On the other hand, sectors prone to the impact of climate related shocks will be given high consideration during the scale up of cPW.

= The scale up of NSDS will be prioritised based on:

i. Stunting prevalence in the District (Data source: DHS) 1

Proportion of households in a District with poor and extremely poor HH (Source: EICV) Proportion of household which are moderately food insecured (Source: CFSVA). Sectors implementing the NSDS will also be required to comply with a number of key ‘readiness criteria’.

0.3. Principles for Payment Delivery and procedures 0.3.1. Principles for payments delivery

Recipients shall receive their full entitlement. In the event of delays to the validation of the targeting list, validated households shall receive payments based on the date the Sector Council approved the enrollment lists;

Payments must be made through formal financial institutions where recipients can choose a payment method most convenient to them (e.g., Bank account, MNO or SACCOs)'; Payments must be made from District accounts at BNR into individual recipients’ accounts. Funds should not flow through Sector accounts.

Payments shall be processed in MEIS by District staff each month for DS, OAG and ePW, each quarter for NSDS and !0 days working cycle for cPVWVW. However, DS and OAG payments for the June payroll shall be approved by District not later than 15" June to allow early targeting process for the following year. The payroll list for other programs should be similarly processed in advance based on their timeline for each payment cycle.

Financial institutions supporting payment delivery should provide a report confirming payment status or reconciliation report to each district/ sector administration after each payment cycle which can be directly uploaded to the MEIS by sector administration during tracking process.

Funds for payments must be transferred to the participating financial institutions in a timely manner, recipients should ideally be able to access their monthly transfers within the month for which they are due and certainly not later than 10 calendar days after the end of that month.

Recipients who cannot travel to their bank/SACCO branch to access payments or who are not allowed to open their own accounts (e.g., those under |8 years of age) may authorise a trusted representative to collect the money on their behalf. This should be recorded in the Minutes of the Annual Targeting List Validation Meeting as per annex I.

0.3.2. Payment procedures Step |: Sector - To create payroll and payroll order from MEIS and forward to District

For cPW intermediary steps to reach step I| are required:

Daily completion of VUP cPW Attendance Sheet by Capitas;

Compilation and approval of Attendance List by Community Representative (every | 0 working days); Endorsement of VUP cPW attendance form by Site Supervisor and submission to Sector;

Sector reviews and endorses attendance lists and authorises MEIS data entry;

Data entry of attendance list data into MEIS by site supervisor (data should beentered within 3 days of completion of the work period)

Sector - To verify data entered by cPW sector supervisor and create payroll from MEIS and forward to District

' For population groups that do not have a transaction account at a formal financial institution, the program should put in place an account opening process with a partner financial institution along with specific activities to build awareness on accessing and using their account.

0.4.2. Planning and budgeting for Public Works schemes (cPW and ePW)

0.4.2.1. Detailed district planning process

Planning Step |: Community-level identification of potential public works projects The selection of public works projects should be driven by a participatory planning process in order to promote the identification and prioritization of community needs. Hence, planning begins with the Umudugudu identifying key outcomes it wishes to achieve and then developing a list of projects that will achieve these outcomes. Bottom-up planning will be complemented by national guidelines and strategic plans (the environmental, agricultural, soil and water management plans). Districts and technical ministries could shortlist together those which are suitable for labour intensive production.

Planning Step 2: Simplified Feasibility Assessments

Simplified feasibility assessments shall be prepared for all VUP Public Works projects i.e cPVV and ePW projects. Ideally these feasibility studies should be prepared well in advance of PBCCI and PPD preparation so that detailed design information is available during the annual planning process. District should work hand in hand with relevant sector ministry as part of their regular planning (e.g. soil or water management plans). These should be prepared by relevant Sector

? In the event that eligibility policy changes and data on the number of eligible households is not available for budgeting purposes, statistical estimates shall be used to set the budget.

4

technical staff (e.g. Sector land manager and infrastructure management officer, Sector Agronomist, Sector Forestry Officer) under supervision of District technical staff (district engineer, agronomist and Forestry). Guidelines for conducting simplified feasibility assessments are provided in Annex 2 for both cPW and ePW. It is important that Simplified Feasibility Assessments provide sufficient information to allow the District Environment Officer to conduct environmental and social screening of the project.

Planning Step 3: Annual selection of PW projects On an annual basis, districts and sectors shall be informed of their eligibility to implement cPVW projects as part of the PBCCI process. In response to PBCCI, Umudugudu shall be asked to identify potential cPW projects which shall be reviewed and consolidated by the Cell and Sector administration. The Sector shall submit its proposed projects to the District for review, approval and integration into the annual District Action Plan. Issues for consideration during prioritization include:

i. | Number of Households employed by ensuring equal benefit between women and men;

ii. Projects attracting woman should be given high priority

iii. | Climate-sensitive and gender smart projects *should be given high priority

iv. Labour-intensity (minimum 70% but the aggregate at District level shall be 80%);

v. | Number of eligible households living close to proposed project site. In selecting the projects, the District must respect national and Development Partners (funding the VUP programme) planning guidelines.

Box 2-Note on trans-Sector (Umurenge) projects

By principal a PW project is implemented within one sector. However, there is a possibility of implementing a big cPW project crossing more than one sector with the purpose of job creation for many people and improving their livelihoods. cPW recipients can only work on the site of the project that runs through their Sector.

A final list of approved projects, which will depend on the budget available, will be posted at Sector offices and communicated to communities through Cell General Assemblies.

1.2. Old Age Grant

1.2.1. Objective

These guidelines aim to explain the implementation of the grant pillar for the elderly in need, with the purpose of improving their well-being and addressing issues associated with old age as outlined in the Vision Umurenge Program (VUP). The individuals referred to in these guidelines are elderly people aged 65 and above living in households with no assets (land, livestock, forests, etc.) or whose assets are too insufficient to sustain their wellbeing.

1.2.2. Fundamental Principals

= The grant for the elderly in need is calculated on an individual basis, not on the household size.

» The criteria for selecting grant recipients must be clear and based on the actual living conditions of the selected individuals during the selection period.

" The purpose of the grant is primarily to address basic and special needs due to old age and any personal life issues arising therefrom; it is strictly for the intended recipients and cannot be transferred to others.

1.2.3. Eligible recipients for the Old Age Grant

The grant is intended for any Rwandan aged 65 and above who is in need and living in the: (a) Households with no labour capacity and with no other source of income

(b) HHs in extremely poor condition and have Only one worker caring for someone with severe disabilities (holding disability card provided by NCPD with category | and 2) or a person with severe disability who has not yet been categorized but demonstrating severe disability proved by community (but the district concerned should keep helping the concerned to get the card)

(c) Household residing in a sector for at least 6 months

Note: A recipient of OAG cannot benefit from other Government direct support programmes (MINUBUMWE, RDRC) or other VUP safety net programmes except NSDS.

2. Expanded Public Works - Labor Based

2.1. Principles of ePW

Expanded public works (ePW) scheme have two main principles that provide a year-round wages

and flexible working hours.

= Wages: Recipients receive a monthly wage of 15,000 Rwf for all 12 months of the year.

» Working hours: Working hours should be flexible. Project outputs should be achievable based on an equivalent of 12 hours’ work per week.

2.2. Selection of Workers and Supervisors for Expanded Public Works

2.2.1. Eligibility criteria

» Households living in extreme poverty / poverty with no assests (lands, livestock, forest, a or the income from those assets is not sufficient to allow the members of households to satisfy their basic needs,

« Residing in a sector for at least 6 months.

2.2.2. Targeting Process

(a) The provisional targeting list (PTL) of ePW recipients is generated from the dynamic social registry and compare it with the previous financial year list of recipients;

(b) Districts and sectors, using the community meeting/ consultation, select the eligible households and adjust the list of eligible households which will be approved by the sector council. Once is approved, the list is entered into the system used to monitor the implementation of this scheme;

(c) Depending on the availability of resources, the eligible households fulfiling the following conditions will be prioritized as follows:

Priority 1: Household with only one member able to work (18<=x<65 years) and at least one child aged from 0 to 5 years;

Priority 2: Household with only one member able to work and has a moderate disability (disability category 3 & 4);

13

Priority 3: Household with only one member able to work and at least one child aged from 6 to 14 years;

Priority 4: Household made of one member who is female and able to work;

Priority 5: Household with only one member able to work and the worker is aged from 60 to 64 years;

Priority 6: Household made of two members able to work and both aged from 60 to 64 years

2.2.3. Selection of Workers (recipients)

A Cell General Assembly meeting should be convened to Identify all eligible households in the cell (per the Provisional Targeting List generated from the Social Registry, review eligible households with people with disability who are not yet medically classified first reviewed and approved by sector disability council level for disability assessment and collect any missing National ID data and bank/SACCO account details for nominated households

2.2.4. Sector Council to approve the final eligible list

Selected households should be presented for approval to the Sector Council by the Sector. Once sector council has approved the eligible list, the Sector should be authorised to enter the data into MEIS.

2.2.5. Finalisation of targeting list The Sector will convene a meeting at sector level for all households selected to participate in the ePW Labor Based project. The purpose of this meeting is to: a) Nominate the ePW workers, elect the group representatives, select the cell supervisors. b) Resolve any discrepancies between project worker requirements, cell quotas and numbers of households selected; c) Inform participants of their roles and responsibilities; d) Collect any participants’ Bank/SACCO account numbers/ MNOs or National ID numbers which were not available during the Cell General Assembly meeting; e) From among the eligible households, identify groups that live close to the proposed work sites; f) Allocate households to ePW projects including Service based and labor based. The process for doing this is likely to vary by location but could operate as follows: Step I: Identify all priority households for ePW C/HBECD Step 2: Enroll all remaining priority households for ePW labor based Step 3: Allocate ePW-eligible HHs (up to the maximum quota size for the sector) to each Labor Based project (10-15 per group depending on proximity to the worksite). Identify group representatives for each Labor based group. e) The Sector Management Team will approve the final list of workers and cell supervisors Households may be enrolled in both ePW LB and ePW H/CBECD projects within the sector in case of insufficient number of participants to deliver to staff the project.

for signature by the District/Sector Executive Secretary.

a) List of final eligible list approved by the Sector Council;

3. ePW-C/HBECD Implementation Guidelines

3.1. Introduction

This section of the document provides detailed guidelines on the planning, implementation and reporting of the VUP ePW Home/Community Based Early Child Development (H/CB-ECD) scheme.

18

The objectives of Expanded Public Works Home/Community-Based Early Childhood Development are: a) To provide multi-year, year-round, accessible and flexible employment for extremely poor households with caring responsibilities. Household with person: i. | who is a woman able to work but pregnant; ii. able to work but having disability (cat 3 & 4 or approved by the community) or illness preventing him/her to work many hours a day; iii, | able to work but constrained by the age between 60 to 64 b) To provide inclusive, safe and stimulating community/ Home Based ECD for children aged 2 to 5 years whilst their parents work. c) Capacity development of caregivers on quality and integrated ECD services delivery for holistic child development and welfare in community of ePW project

3.2. Eligibility criteria and priorities

The Households that are eligible for Home Based ECDs are selected from the eligible list of

ePW recipients starting by the caregivers who received previously training on ECD services

delivery, but following the criteria below depending on the resources availability:

= Priority !: Household with only one member able to work and at least one child aged between 0 and 5 years;

# Priority 2: Household with only one member able to work, who is disabled but can still work (disability category 3 & 4);

= Priority 3: Households with only one member able to work and at least one child aged between 6 and 1/4 years;

= Priority 4: Household made of only one member who is female;

# Priority 5: Households with only one member able to work and aged between 60 and 64 years;

# Priority 6: Households made of only two members able to work and aged between 60 and 64 years;

« Priority 7: Teen mothers impregnated early but have 16 years old today if she is not at school as they are first encouraged to return to school;

Note: Under no circumstances shall children under the age of I6 years (teen/young girls) be

allowed to work in C/HB ECD.

3.3. Selection of ePW C/HBECD participants

3.3.1. Selection of Caregivers

On average, each H/CBECD project should employ | lead care-giver who sign contract (see annex 21) on behalf of other additional 6 assistant care-givers (ePW recipients). H/CBECD are expected to provide care for approximately 10-15 children. This is in line with the ECD minimum standards of the teacher child ratio between the age ranges of 2-5 years old.

There are expected to be between 10-20 C/HB ECDs per sector, although the exact number will depend on the number of ePW recipients in each sector.

3.3.2. Responsibilities of caregivers Caregivers will be responsible for:

19

Ensuring a safe environment and providing stimulating care for the children. Caregivers will develop a weekly schedule that will include educational activities and games, singing and supervised play.

Preparing daily porridge or food for the children in accordance with nutrition guidelines and provide safe drinking water, supervising toileting and ensuring that the latrine and general environment is kept clean and safe at all times.

Facilitating the weekly distribution of FBF to primary care givers (parents/guardians) of children below 3 years of age.

Facilitating referrals of sick children to nearby health facilities or community health workers (CHW).

Facilitating referrals and follow-up of children suffering abuse to the Inshuti z’umuryango (IZU).

Facilitating monthly visits by CHW for the screening of children and Parenting education in health aspects (nutrition, cooking demonstrations, family planning, vaccination...) in collaboration with IZU and CHW and other ECD partners.

Facilitating referrals of services for children with disabilities and special needs in collaboration with the IZU and CHW.

Facilitating the setting up of the kitchen garden for the home based ECD nutrition services and cooking demonstration and monitor its maintenance.

Facilitating referrals through the para social workers for eligible social protection recipients to the Cell Social and development (SEDO)/ Human Capital Development officer. Collaborate with the cell SEDO on administrative and management issues, and refer any more difficult technical issues to the Sector Supervisor.

Mobilise Parents to enrol their children in ECD Settings

3.3.3. Selection of lead caregivers The Lead Caregivers shall be selected among the caregivers preferably the owner of the C/HBECD facility and should fullfil at least the following requirements:

» Able to read and write = Of good conduct, honest, able to organise and supervise work at C/HBECD facility.

On behalf of the group of caregivers, lead caregivers should sign a contract with District/ Sector.

3.3.4. Days and Hours of Operation of the C/HBECD and Required Facilities

Home-based care will operate all year round up to 6 days per week and for approximately 5 to 6 hours per day.

The precise hours of operation will be agreed collectively by the C/HB ECD care givers and sector supervisor in order to cover the working hours of parents, with particular attention given to covering the working hours of parents reliant on agricultural wage work.

ePW H/CBECD Facilities will provide care for children between 2 to 5 years. Community/ Home Based ECD is not suitable for children younger than 24 months due to their significant care needs and the need for the youngest children to remain close to mothers for breastfeeding. At the age of 5 where a pre-primary class are available, they should be encouraged to attend and at age of 6 years’ children must enroll in the local primary school. Each C/HBECD Facility will cover one or two villages, depending on the number of eligible and interested households. First priority will be given to children from households’ recipients of VUP. If there are any remaining spaces, other children in proximity will be enrolled in the

center. When a child leaves to go to pre-primary or primary school the caregivers will seek a replacement. The same entry criteria will be followed.

The percentage of poor households should be regularly monitored, and in every C/HB ECD facility at least 50% of the children should be from poor households.

HB-ECD Setting

Children will be cared for close to their own homes in either a home of one of the parents or in an available community/ public building.

The home owner is by principle a lead caregiver, unless he/she has other attributions which conflict with that responsibility.

Parents might also decide to construct a purpose-built centre, using their own resources or with the support of local authorities or another partner. Although, the funds may not be used for construction of a C/HBECD site, minor refurbishments of existing facilities may be financed under this arrangement. This may include the light construction or renovation of a latrine where necessary.

If a chosen site is a home, the owner should be priotirised and encouraged to provide care in their home for a period of at least three years. However, the duration of hosting this chosen site should not be an imposition on the home owner. If, in exceptional circumstances, it proves necessary for the group to move before the end of the three-year period, all equipment provided to the group must be moved also, the home owner should sign a concent form

In this case: Sector should contract with the home owner and perform the refurbishment activities, the funds dedicated to these activities are determined and prioritised by the sector and should not exceed RWF 250,000. If it requires more, the site should be rejected and replaced by another home. The opinion leaders are preferred if they agreed to host the C/HBECD.

4. Nutrition Sensitive Direct Support scheme

4.1. Introduction

The Government of Rwanda through Ministry of Local Government and Local Administrative Entities Development Agency (LODA) which has been an umbrella for social protection programs in Rwanda (GoR), decided to act proactively by investing in the early years of a child’s life with the broader objective to contribute to the eradication of malnutrition (SP plan 2018- 2024) hence contribute to human capital development. In the same framework, the GoR through LODA with support from different partners’ implements Nutrition Sensitive Direct Support (NSDS) under VUP program. Originally established as a flagship programme within the first Economic Development and Poverty Reduction Strategy (EDPRS), the VUP remains key to the delivery of a range of national targets under EDPRS2 (2013-2018) and realisation of Vision 2050.

NSDS has the following two main objectives:

= To provide income support to poor and vulnerable pregnant women, new mothers and young children, allowing for adequate consumption and improved diet; and

= To incentivize uptake of key health and nutrition services and behavioural change.

Specific objectives

» Prevent and eliminate stunting and malnutrition among under five children by providing support to poor households allowing them to access on improved diet and appropriate use of key health and nutrition services;

= Prevent malnutrition and/or stunting associated with lack of hygiene and sanitation by facilitating and incentivizing good practices among recipients; Encourage/incentivize recipients (pregnant mothers, mother’s wit under two children and caregivers) for uptake of key health

28

and nutrition services provided by health centers and community health workers (Family planning included);

4.2. Target group and eligibility criteria

4.2.1. Target groups

All pregnant woman or a child aged 0-24 months from Households living in extreme poverty/ poverty with no assests (lands, livestock, forest, ...) or the available income from those assets is not sufficient to allow households access on the basic needs and/ or practices adequate nutrition of child in the 1000 days of life, shall be eligible for the NSDS cash transfers.

4.2.2. Eligibility criteria:

= Pregnant women and children aged 0-24 months living in poor households determined as socially vulnerable according to the relevant socio-economic and welfare criteria (limited assets or other source of income to sustain their livelihoods), who are permanent residents of the targeted sector.

Note!: Households benefitting from other social protection and nutrition interventions shall

remain eligible for the NSDS transfers.

Note2: A child not registered in the NSDS scheme within 18 months of birth shall not qualify.

4.2.3. Registration mechanism

At community level, community members are informed about the core objectives of the NSDS

by community health workers, parasocial workers and local authorities (cell executive secretary,

and SEDOs);

# Eligible Recipients will be identified by local governments on an on-going basis in partnership with health centers;

» Eligible pregnant women should be identified early on in pregnancy and payments will continue until their child’s second birthday;

# Eligible pregnant women and/or mothers /guardians will be encouraged to attend their local health center for routine ANC, PNC and nutrition services use;

= After the use of maternal/or child health services (ANC, PNC, growth monitoring services), detailed health data together with ID number of the pregnant women, mother and/or guardian, will be entered into HMIS-Nutrition App by the health center staff ’ providing services to the candidate;

® In the event that a pregnant woman, mother/guardian does not have an NID card, will be oriented to his/her local authority (SEDO) for facilitation;

= A youth (volunteer) allocated to each NSDS implementing health center will be facilitating and monitoring quality and regularity of data entry at health center level on regular basis;

» MEIS will retrieve data of potential recipients from the Health management information system (HMIS)/Nutrition module and will therefore maintain a dynamic list of possible recipients that tracks the age of children and incorporates newly pregnant eligible women as and when they are identified;

= Recorded recipients’ data will be automatically transferred into LODA MEIS to allow sector staff to recognize potential NSDS recipients on time;

» The health center staff may be a nurse, midwife and nutritionist depending on the utilized health unit 29

The health center data managers will be checking quality assurance of data transferred into MEIS (missing preconditions) on a weekly basis, identify all cases missing preconditions and then share the report with concerned staff at health center/or sector level for correction;

On quarterly basis (September, December, March and June), the sector management meeting will review, update and approve the list of NSDS recipients, imported from HMIS, based on socio economic conditions of each household recipients;

The sector will submit the approved payrolls (maintained into the system) to the District and request for cash transfer.

4.2.4. Payment principles and procedures 4.2.4.1. Payment Principles

NSDS recipients are entitled to RVWFI0,000 per month, transferred on a quarterly basis (30,000Rwf), irrespective of whether a pregnant woman also has an eligible child or whether a mother has more than one eligible child or whether a guardian has more than one eligible child, except a mother with twins who will get a double monthly payment of Rwf1!0,000. Twins'° should be treated differently from other children to promote birth spacing and other good family planning practices;

Payments shall be made directly by district to the pregnant woman or mother/guardian of the eligible infant. Eligible women/mothers/guardians are entitled to open their personal accounts with their local formal financial institutions for this purpose;

Entitlements will be calculated for each full month after the date of enrolment i.e. if an individual enrols mid-month (after 15" of the month), their entitlement shall commence from the beginning of the following month;

No deductions whatsoever shall be made from NSDS payments during the payment process except on recipient order supported by a signed individual consent.

Payments must be made to the recipients not later than 10" (calendar days) of the first month following the quarter under consideration i.e 10 of October, January, April and

July;"!

The NSDS is specifically intended to support consumption among extremely poor pregnant women and children. 6. Grievance Redress Mechanism and Case Management

40

6.1. Overview and objectives

The Grievance Redressal and Case Management system is a social accountability mechanism that will be used to foster a collaborative approach in receiving, addressing, and responding to the different social protection programs related complaints and appeals.

It will be part of the project implementation, and aims to:

* Resolve all grievances emanating from the project activities in a timely manner;

= Establish relationships of trust between project staff and stakeholders and Guide project stakeholders (i.e. MINALOC, LODA, Districts, Social protection partners and recipients) on their key functions, roles and responsibilities to better implement the project;

= Create transparency among stakeholders including affected persons through an established communication system;

= Booster the relationship of trust amongst the project staff and the affected parties

= Enable recipients and non-recipients to understand the procedures to lodge appeals, complaints as well as mechanisms for updates

6.2. Types of grievances/ appeals/ feedback There are various grievances that are likely to occur during the implementation of social protection projects. In accordance with Social Protection Programs, grievances/ appeals or forms of feedback that might arise include the Grievance related: * To lack of information about the projects * To errors in recipient data = To exclusion from the registration process i.e exclusion from social protection program * To lack of assistance for individuals with disabilities " To recipients entitllemnt (left off the payroll, receiving wrong amount, having fees deducted illegitimately) * To social protection program staff who disrespect or abuse the program recipients = To Gender Based Violance, sexual exploitation and abuse, harassment ® To all forms of discrimination, corruption, and fraud ® To construction works including such as noise, dust, vibration, workers’ disputes among others " To land acquisition, land boundaries, valuation/compensation, income or livelihood loss; = Misuse of funds; * Complaints about project staff; « Complaints about procurement by contractors; = Complaints about Contractor performance

6.3. Grievance redress mechanism process Following are procedures for initiating, handling, monitoring and providing feedback or complaintsthat may rise during the implementation social protection programs:

6.3.1. Initiation

This step refers to the methods by which the project will collect grievances or feedback. It includesuptake locations and channels, language for submission, form for filing grievances as well as filer and receiver of the grievances.

41

Location for filing complaint or feedback Communities and/or individuals who believe that have grievances, complaints, questions, feedback or suggestions may submit them at any level of the project implementation structure: = At cell, sector and district levels where project is being implemented; » AtLODA » At MINALOC Channels for raising complaints or feedback All complaints or feedback can be channeled via: = Phone calls, Text messages, =» Meetings, Proactive outreach, a Letters, Verbal narration, = Emails, Suggestion, boxes, « —_ Reports on visits to project sites by project staff, independent monitors, supervision team, government » Findings of World Bank supervision and implementation support missions » WB’s Grievance Redress Service (GRS) as well as WB's independent Inspection Panel # Call in questions, comments, or complaints from radio programs * Media articles or other publications

Language for raising/submission of complaint or feedback Individuals and community members may submit feedback/grievances in the language of their choice.

Form for filing the complaint/feedback The feedback or grievance can be given orally or in writing (soft or hard copies). Whenever possible and for documentation purposes, grievance redress form (annex 21) will be used.

Transmitter of the complaint/feedback Anyone shall be able to lodge a complaint and different methods (in person, telephone, email, social media, website, forms written in local language, suggestion boxes, among others) shall be used. They can also report their concerns through third parties and provided communication channels should not inhibit the lodgment of any complaint. Here are some tips that can be used by the GRC in order to encourage community members to submit complaints: # Ensuring that communication materials are prepared and distributed widely to raise awareness of the existence of the GRM; ® Ensuring that it is free to report a complaint; = Ensure Members of the Grievance Redress Committee are trained on how to receive and address the complaints; * Confidentiality of complainants is observed; = GRC members should help potential complainants write their grievances if needed. Receiver of the complaint/feedback Generally, Grievance Redress Committees and all project staff are authorized to receive grievances. Particularly, grievances that will be sent via: # Emails will be received by the staff involved in project implementation; 42

= Hotline, media and articles will be received by the Communication team of MINALOC and LODA

6.3.2. Sort and Process

Various types of grievances/feedback typically require different follow-up actions i.e some can

be resolved by means of a simple explanation or apology, while others may require more

extensive investigations. Therefore, this is the step where Grievence Rsdress Committee

categorize grievances, assign priority, and route them to the appropriate entity. However, all

forms of grievance have to be recorded with specific dates and how they were resolved and

reported to the LODA and MINALOC.

6.3.2.1. Categorization

Grievances and feedback can be categorized in four main types:

= Comments, suggestions, or queries;

# Complaints relating to nonperformance of project obligations;

" Complaints referring to violations of law and/or corruption; and

= Complaints against program staff or community members involved in program management.

6.3.2.2. Prioritization

There is a need to assess urgency and prioritize the complaints to know which need to be addressedimmediately or if they can follow the provided timeframe for redress. Cases that need to be addressed immediately include GBV cases, health incidents, fraud and corruption among others.

Issues to consider may include:

» What effect does it have on an individual’s or a group’s welfare and safety?

# Does it have the negative impact on the implementation timeframe of the project?

= |s it attracting much public attention?

6.3.2.3. Assigning appropriate entity After prioritization, grievances should be directed to the appropriate entity/ GRC for resolution.

# Complaints related to NSDS Scheme Complaints related to incorrect or incomplete data within the Nutrition App- FBF MIS (which has resulted in exclusion or suspension of payments) shall be directed, in the first instance, to the health center. In the event that the health center fails to address the complaint satisfactorily, the recipient may escalate their complaint to the Sector that shall be responsible for investigating and engaging the health center on behalf of the recipient and ensuring that data is corrected and that feedback is provided to the recipient.

The Sector Social Affairs Officer should keep a register within MEIS (appeals and complains

module) of all grievances received, track their resolution and ensure that feedback is provided to the persons submitting the complaint. In the event that it is impossible to resolve a grievance

43

at sector level, the Sector or the complainant themselves should escalate the issue to the district, which should investigate the case and advise the Sector accordingly. In the event that local government fails to resolve a grievance satisfactorily and within a reasonable period of time, the complaint should be escalated to MINALOC or LODA. However, the final authorities on eligibility for the NSDS scheme shall remain with the Sector and health center.

# Grievance Channel for Gender-Based Violence When GBV related complaint including sexual harassment is received at the first or second tier of GRM, the complaint should be kept confidential by the person receiving the complaint, and the complaint will be immediately reported to Isange One Stop Center (IOSC), a national police-led center established to provide comprehensive support to victims of GBV. The complaint should be reported to the relevant committee and immediate actions should be taken that is consistent with the wishes and choices, rights and dignity of the complainant. The complainant should be given information in simple and clear terms on the steps for filing complaints and the possible outcomes, the timelines and the types of supports available to be able to make informed decision. For GBV cases, it is important to ensure that access to the complaints processes is as easy and as safe as possible for the complainant survivor. The recording of incidence should be limited to the nature of complaint put exactly in the words of the complainant, the age of the survivor and if to the best of their knowledge, the perpetrator was associated with the program. The complainant should decide on whether they would like to be referred to the grievance committee and the complainant should give consent to share basic monitoring data.

" Court of law If GRM failed to resolve the complaints, the Complainant will have the right to take the matter to the appropriate legal or judicial authority as per Rwanda National Legal procedure. However, the complainant is adivised to start by mediation committee (Abunzi) if the complaint falls under this structure.

6.3.3. Acknowledge and follow up

When a complaint is made, the GRC should acknowledge its receipt in a communication that outlines the grievance process; provides contact details and, if possible, the name of the contact person who is responsible for handling the grievance; and notes how long it is likely to take to resolve the grievance. Complainants will then receive periodic updates on the status of their grievances in the stipulated time for resolution as per the duration provided.

6.3.4. Investigate and act This step involves gathering information about the grievance to determine its validity, and resolving the grievance.

6.3.4.1. Verification and Investigation

This step includes gathering of facts and clarifying information in order to have a clear picture of the circumstances surrounding the grievance or complaint. In this regard, the GRC should

44

decide which team members will be responsible for fact-finding and should ensure that they are

neutral and do not have any stake in the outcome of the investigation.

The selected members should try to:

» Identify the parties involved in the case

» Clear issues and concerns raised by the complaint

" Collect views of other stakeholders, including those of social protection programs if needed

6.3.4.2. Taking action

This step reflects the action and decision-making process that will be done at different levels. The GRC should be open to looking at different ways to addressing grievances. In general, the GRCs should review existing resolution methods when deciding on which way to appropriate response. Where applicable, even traditional methods that are fair should be applied. Potential action includes either:

= Responding to a query, comment or suggestion, imposing sanctions or

# Referring the grievance to an appropriate level of the system

Note that the GRCs should resolve a complaint when it is straightforward, the issue is clear and that it can be resolved at that level.

6.3.5. Monitor and evaluate

This step refers to the process of tracking grievances and assessing the extent to which progress is being made to resolve them. In order to assess the extent to which the desired objectives of GRM are being realized and inform decision making in case adjustments that might be needed in handling complaints raising due to social protection program implementation, the key performance indicators are:

(i) Number of grievances received,

(ii) Percentage of grievance resolved, and

(iii) | Percentage of grievances redressed within stipulated time period.

In order to measure those indicators, a Complaints/Feedback Register will be maintained by the committees and safeguards staff, who will log the:

(i) details and nature of the complaint; (ii) the complainant name and their contact details; (iii) | date; and

(iv) corrective actions taken in response to the complaint. Specifically, the following indicator of the social protection program’s results framework will be utilized: Grievances registered related to delivery of project benefits that are addressed. This indicator will measure the utilization of GRM by reporting on percentage of number addressed grievances in comparison to number of grievances received.

6.3.6. Provide feedback

This step refers to the process of informing the complainant or aggrieved party about the status of the complaint or provision of information required by the project stakeholder. Any questions, procedures, findings and proposed actions should be translated into a language that

45

the complainant understands. Depending on the nature of complaints, the GRC should decide on the appropriate means of communication. If the complainant is known, feedback can be provided directly. GRM users will be informed about their right to an appeal if they are dissatisfied with the decision. The complainant may file an appeal to the next higher level of the grievance redress system or to an appropriate office. The national Grievance Redress Committee is the highest level in the GRM and their decision is final, unless the complainant wishes to take their grievance to other relevant government agencies (e.g., courts, Office of the Ombudsman, etc.)

6.3.7. Confidentiality in Grievance Documentation

Records of grievances including information such as gender, age and location, makes grievance management easier. This information, however, should be protected from unauthorized people and the GRCs should always protect complainants’ confidentiality.

6.2. Establishment of Grievance Redress Committee (GRC)

During social protection program implementation, people may be affected during the project or not gain the benefits that they were expected to obtain from the project. To better address the related complaints, it is planned to established grievance redress committee with a purpose of resolving complaints and dissatisfaction of program affected people in a timely manner.

6.2.1. National level Grievance Redress Committee (GRC)

The national GRC will be established at LODA and made up with different staff implementing each scheme (classic Public Works, expended Public Works, Direct Support, Nutrition Sensitive Direct Support) communication and safeguard teams. Complainants should submit their complaints through emails, hotline or social media platform: E-mail: info@loda.gov.rw, Hotline: 5029, Tweet: @LODARwanda.

The grievance redress committee will be composed of the following members: Chairperson: The chairperson of the committee is the Division Manager of community development starategy and coordination division and has the following responsibilities: = Invite and chair meetings of the committee, =» Communicate the action required from responsible parties for effecting the agreed upon responses; # Serve as the spokesperson of the committee and report to the LODA management and its partners. Co-chair person The co chairs of the committee are the Program managers and Director of Social Protection Unit with the following responsibilities: = Invite and chair the meeting in the absence of the chairperson; = Assist the President in giving directions during meeting discussions; = Serve as the spokesperson of the GRC and report to the LODA management and its partners in the absence of the President.

46

Secretary

The secretariate of the committee is a team composed by social safeguard team and communication team. While Social Safeguard specialist will lead the team and serve as minutes’ taker during the meetings and filing all documents related to the GRM, Communication staff will receive and record grievance received through social media and hotline in a logbook.

Level II: District Level GRC

The committee will be composed of the following members namely the Vice Mayor in Charge of Social Affairs as Chairperson, Director of Social Development Unit as co chair, District Social Protection Officer who will be the Secretary, the District Environmental Officer, a Women Representative from the Unit of Social Protection who will also represent youth and vulnerable groups will be member of the committee. Though not part of the committee, Isange One Stop Centre Representative will participate in District GRC meetings whenever deemed necessary.

6.2.3. Facilitation of GRC’s members

GRCs members will be trained at least once a quarter and will be entitled to a transportation allowance of 10,000rwfs as a facilitation to attend the training. In case there is a need to train the GRCs members from different District outside their respective Districts, in addition to the transportation allowances, they will be entitled to mission allowances. The mission allowances will be calculated basing on Ministerial Instructions N°001/15/10/TC of 20/07/2015 determining mission fees of public servants on official mission inside the country. In this case Grievance committee members will be considered as civil servant of level J/4, K/5 and L/6. Their respective Districts will provide them with Travel clearances and LODA/Districts, MINALOC or any other organizing/inviting institution among the social protection stakeholders will pay the mission allowances and transport.

Grounds the Direct Support household entitlement table: number of household members 1 pays FRW 7,500; 2 pays 12,000; 3 pays 15,000; 4 pays 18,000; 5 or more pays 21,000 - monthly transfers, framed by the manual's own text on monthly payment access. The RWAMOD v2.0 country report (May 2026) states this schedule is unchanged as of July 2025, so it stands for the CY2025 validation year. VUP Direct Support is administrative (LODA/MINALOC); no gazetted instrument fixes the schedule. Flag: scanned print, fresh 350 dpi OCR.
